summary: Muratori argues that all patents, but especially software patents, are fundamentally bad law with no empirical support for their existence. He explains that patents are not about preventing theft but about forcing people to pay for ideas even when independently conceived. Through a detailed email originally written to a Stanford Law student, he lays out six arguments against software patents: they're unusable in practice due to sheer volume, copyright already handles software licensing effectively, trivial concepts get patented while important ones don't, no one can define 'patent quality,' the pre-patent era was more innovative, and software patents raise First Amendment concerns. He concludes that after years of research and reading every Supreme Court case on the subject, he has found no evidence software patents are useful.
key_points: Patents are not about preventing theft — they force people to pay for ideas even when independently conceived, with triple damages only if the infringer knew about the patent
With over 40,000 software patents issued yearly, no programmer can know a priori whether they're infringing, making the system function purely as an uncertainty settlement market
Copyright already provides effective software licensing without patents — Muratori's own career earnings came from licensing unpatented software
Virtually everything we interact with on computers today was conceived before software patents existed in the pre-1990 era, suggesting patents haven't driven innovation
Software patents raise First Amendment issues because software is a medium of personal expression, making them analogous to patenting phrases in English
money_quotes: Patents are not about protecting intellectual property from theft — that is an almost negligible part of the law.
It's about forcing people to pay for the use of patented ideas even when they came up with the idea themselves.

Almost the entire practical result from software patents is to create an uncertainty settlement market, whereby software developers pay patent holders after the fact to avoid entanglement in lawsuits.
It makes no sense to say that you need to 'fix the system' when none of the great stuff required it! Just get rid of the system, and you're done.
Whenever you sit down at a computer today, it is highly likely you will interact exclusively with things that are modified versions of something that existed in 1975.
Allowing patents on software is therefore much more similar to allowing patents on phrases or sentences in English, and raises all the same concerns.
I have found literally no evidence to ever suggest that software patents are useful, and plenty to show that they are actively harmful.
And I've yet to hear a coherent argument to the contrary that didn't fall apart immediately under a moment's scrutiny.
key_insight: Software patents don't protect innovation — they create an uncertainty settlement market that taxes independent invention, while all the foundational software innovations were created without them.
